text:
I just added three 256-color color schemes.
1. Do the attached screenshots have any low-contrast bits?
2. Could you try them out on your side, see if they look different, or if you spot any more subtle issues? You'll need to tweak `COLOR_SCHEME` at <https://github.com/akkartik/teliva/blob/main/src/teliva.h|src/teliva.h> and then rerun `make` for your platform (`make linux`, `make macosx`, etc.)
(The 'solarized' isn't really, more just a sense of where I'm getting my inspiration. The default 256-color palette isn't really good enough for Solarized.)

text: Well, I would be willing to have a proper *video meeting* wherein we as a community attempt to compare and contrast our perspectives on "the problem" with programming, and possibly come up with an "official" document that captures them. Perhaps we could try this? If you're interested, add your availability <https://www.when2meet.com/?13851953-J31FY|to this poll>.

root.text:
Has this community ever agreed on some "big problems" in programming language design? Having recently gone back to think about "big picture" things, I've realised I have no idea what other people think is important. Surely there are a couple of historical threads.

It would be a little awkward if after several years of having this Slack group, we've never collectively stopped to define the problems we're working on.

text:
Something for all the minimalist and retro-computing fans:
<https://twitter.com/jackrusher/status/1468179654066515968?s=20>

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/jackrusher|@jackrusher>: I thought it'd be fun to recreate the first Truchet tiling I ever saw:

10 PRINT CHR$(205.5+RND(1)); : GOTO 10 

... as a standalone x86 operating system.

Assembly source code and instructions to make a #BootableTruchet floppy to try with qemu:

<https://gist.github.com/jackrusher/0b087a2c29db2a0d69d0c2e381d1048b> <https://pbs.twimg.com/media/FGADYJZXsAU1VEe.png>

When we let programmers structure the world through code, the strictures of the code's language frame what's possible, and therefore also the world they create and impose upon us

text:
<@UJBAJNFLK> Thanks for the file! Your bug is a doozie:
• Start Teliva with any .tlv program
• Hit `ctrl-e` to edit
• Select any function
• Position the cursor on any Lua keyword. The cursor in the editor starts out on the word `function`, which will do.
• Hit `ctrl-g` and jump to the word at the cursor. You'll now see a blank editor.
...and you're already doomed. All ways to go elsewhere or run the program will drop a definition for the keyword into the image file. And running with any such definition causes Teliva to just silently quit.

Now trying to understand why this happens.

elements.text: Thanks for the file! Your bug is a doozie:
Start Teliva with any .tlv program
Hit
ctrl-e
to edit
Select any function
Position the cursor on any Lua keyword. The cursor in the editor starts out on the word
function
, which will do.
Hit
ctrl-g
and jump to the word at the cursor. You'll now see a blank editor.

...and you're already doomed. All ways to go elsewhere or run the program will drop a definition for the keyword into the image file. And running with any such definition causes Teliva to just silently quit.

Now trying to understand why this happens.

text: I probably need a way to exit the editor without saving. Protections against losing data are too strong. Ugh, UX is hard.

elements.text: I probably need a way to exit the editor without saving. Protections against losing data are too strong. Ugh, UX is hard.

text: makes me think of Dan Ingalls’s definition of an OS: “An operating system is a collection of things that don’t fit into a language. There shouldn’t be one.”

text: Howdy folks, I'm yet, I use they them pronouns, and have been programming for a while but recently dug into the podcast cause of the Orca/100r episodes, big indieweb nerd (Elliott Cost is to blame) and I guess that world and this one are kinda a Venn diagram of sorts. Interested in generative/experimental social experience and data visualization (D3!) And urban Ag tech and racket and looking back to look forward. New to the future of coding so I will be more of a listener but I'm so glad to have found y'all :relaxed::relaxed::non-potable_water:

text: Tbh, I don't think the problem with programmers structuring the world is the programming languages they use! :blush:

elements.text: Tbh, I don't think the problem with programmers structuring the world is the programming languages they use!

text: The author working in "computational law", I guess he considers any programming language (perhaps even any formal language) too constraining for structuring the world. And with that I'd agree.

elements.text: The author working in "computational law", I guess he considers any programming language (perhaps even any formal language) too constraining for structuring the world. And with that I'd agree.

text:
Yeah, definitely not the only problem. But a small problem that is part of this larger problem. 

Imagine if for example McCarthy's elephant 2000 existed. Now we have a language involving speech acts. A different conceptual framework than I think many of us programmers are familiar with.

As we structure the world more and more through code, we do have to ask what sorts of assumptions are pre-baked into our languages. What things are easy to express? What things are hard? Does that fact that most of our programming languages today are fairly similar have any impact on that?

elements.text:
Yeah, definitely not the only problem. But a small problem that is part of this larger problem. 

Imagine if for example McCarthy's elephant 2000 existed. Now we have a language involving speech acts. A different conceptual framework than I think many of us programmers are familiar with.

As we structure the world more and more through code, we do have to ask what sorts of assumptions are pre-baked into our languages. What things are easy to express? What things are hard? Does that fact that most of our programming languages today are fairly similar have any impact on that?

text:
So as a full time clojure programmer I definitely have a lot of opinions on this. I largely agree with the philosophy, but at the same time find some unease with it. 

I guess I want to have this conversation, but I also don't want to be super negative. 

Clojure is a fantastic set of tradeoffs that makes for a super productive programming environment. But I do worry that there is a lot of talk from rich that this model of programming is the only good one. That is something I think needs more push back in the clojure world. 

I think languages have something to learn from clojure. But clojure could learn a lot from other languages as well.

elements.text:

I guess I want to have this conversation, but I also don't want to be super negative. 

Clojure is a fantastic set of tradeoffs that makes for a super productive programming environment. But I do worry that there is a lot of talk from rich that this model of programming is the only good one. That is something I think needs more push back in the clojure world. 

I think languages have something to learn from clojure. But clojure could learn a lot from other languages as well.

text:
The languages are rarely the problem (unless we go Sapir-Whorf here), but the problem is the models we express in those language and how we stick to them or not.

Put differently, the problem is not that your language has Booleans, it's when you use Boolean to model gender when you create a problem.

elements.text:
The languages are rarely the problem (unless we go Sapir-Whorf here), but the problem is the models we express in those language and how we stick to them or not.

Put differently, the problem is not that your language has Booleans, it's when you use Boolean to model gender when you create a problem.

text: Exactly. Inappropriate and/or premature formalization. Which happens not only in code. The numerical targets so loved by managers are quite similar.

elements.text: Exactly. Inappropriate and/or premature formalization. Which happens not only in code. The numerical targets so loved by managers are quite similar.

text:
Completely agree that models are the problem. I do think I'd just add to that that what models are easy or difficult to work with and express depends on our language. 

Simple examples, Nats vs u32 vs integer vs floats. Some problems are better modeled with these various types. But it I'm in J's I'm probably modeling them with floats (the only real number type).

How many things do we not do or choose to not even consider because our languages make it difficult to express?
